topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. Dust motes danced in the afternoon sun slanting across the mahogany desk where Mr. Silas used to sit. I stood by the door, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame, the air thick and suffocating. He was gone. Not just on vacation, but vanished. No note, no call, just an empty chair and a calendar marked with his last day, now a ghost. A sharp, unpleasant clench tightened in my chest, a physical knot that made my breath catch. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but the sound caught in my throat, lodging there like grit. I walked to his desk, my steps deliberate and heavy, and ran a finger over the smooth, cool surface of the wood. It felt mocking, this stillness.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed, the words scraping out of my mouth like broken glass. My voice sounded thin, reedy, and utterly devoid of warmth. I watched the receptionist, her practiced, placid smile seeming to mock my agitation. Every muscle in my jaw was tense, pulled tight enough to ache. I felt a burning sensation behind my eyes, a hot pressure that made focusing on anything else impossible. He had simply evaporated. No farewell, no acknowledgment of the years of guidance, just an abrupt cessation of presence. I turned sharply, the sound of my heels clicking against the marble floor echoing the sudden, jarring emptiness inside me. I didn't look back.

[story 3]

I found the framed photograph on his shelf—a smiling, younger Silas, radiating an easy confidence I could no longer access. My fingers traced the glass, feeling the coldness seep into my skin. A profound sense of betrayal washed over me, a bitter tide rising in my stomach. It wasn't just the loss of a mentor; it was the sudden realization that all the effort, the late nights, the unwavering belief I had poured into him, had been utterly inconsequential to him. I let out a slow, ragged exhale, the air tasting metallic and sour. I didn't cry; tears felt too weak for the magnitude of this disappointment. I simply turned and walked out, the door closing with a soft, definitive click that sealed the finality of his absence.
